coredata.dc:description: Micro short circuit (MSC) fault diagnosis is thought functional in preventing thermal runaway of lithium-ion battery packs. Inconsistencies in the initial state-of-charge and aging state inevitably exist among cells of a battery pack. The existing method for MSC diagnosis disregards the symptoms originating from cell-to-cell inconsistency, which may lead to misdiagnosing inconsistent cells as MSC cells and vice versa. This work presents a method for detecting and quantitatively diagnosing MSC faults in lithium-ion battery packs, while taking cell inconsistency into consideration. Initially, the median incremental capacity (IC), derived based on ranking the terminal voltages of cells, is used as a benchmark representing the state of normal cells. Subsequently, the correlation coefficients between the ICs of individual cells and their median IC are calculated in both the time and frequency domains, as to distinguish the normal, inconsistent, and MSC cells. After detecting the MSC cell, an algorithm, which is based on a recursive least squares algorithm with forgetting factor and an adaptive H ∞ Kalman filtering, is designed to calculate the short-circuit resistance online. The experimental results demonstrate that the short-circuit resistance estimated by the proposed algorithm exhibits rapid convergence to the actual values, thereby confirming the utility of the proposed algorithm in real-life contexts.

head.ce:title: Detection and quantitative diagnosis of micro-short-circuit faults in lithium-ion battery packs considering cell inconsistency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Micro short circuit (MSC) fault diagnosis is thought functional in preventing thermal runaway of lithium-ion battery packs. Inconsistencies in the initial state-of-charge and aging state inevitably exist among cells of a battery pack. The existing method for MSC diagnosis disregards the symptoms originating from cell-to-cell inconsistency, which may lead to misdiagnosing inconsistent cells as MSC cells and vice versa. This work presents a method for detecting and quantitatively diagnosing MSC faults in lithium-ion battery packs, while taking cell inconsistency into consideration. Initially, the median incremental capacity (IC), derived based on ranking the terminal voltages of cells, is used as a benchmark representing the state of normal cells. Subsequently, the correlation coefficients between the ICs of individual cells and their median IC are calculated in both the time and frequency domains, as to distinguish the normal, inconsistent, and MSC cells. After detecting the MSC cell, an algorithm, which is based on a recursive least squares algorithm with forgetting factor and an adaptive H ∞ Kalman filtering, is designed to calculate the short-circuit resistance online. The experimental results demonstrate that the short-circuit resistance estimated by the proposed algorithm exhibits rapid convergence to the actual values, thereby confirming the utility of the proposed algorithm in real-life contexts.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advantages of high power density, low self-discharge rate, and long cycle life, lithium-ion batteries have been widely used in the fields of electric vehicles and power grid energy storage [1]. However, lithium-ion batteries are subject to potential safety concerns, particularly in the form of accidents triggered by thermal runaways, which have hampered the wide public confidence in deploying lithium-ion batteries in large scales [2]. Over the past few years, analysis reports of spontaneous combustion accidents of battery packs from different research institutes have revealed that internal short circuit (ISC) is one of the main causes of thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries [3–6].

ISCs in lithium-ion batteries are usually triggered by mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuses [7]. Mechanical abuse, such as collision, extrusion, or punctures, can damage the battery structure and cause the battery to suffer severe deformation, which in turn may lead to an electrical connection between the positive and negative electrodes and thus trigger a short circuit inside the battery [8]. Thermal abuse can provoke large-scale collapse and shrinkage of the battery's separator, resulting in direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes, and thus the consequent short circuit. Electrical abuse can cause metals such as lithium to precipitate and deposit on the negative electrode, thereby forming lithium dendrites. The growing lithium dendrites may pierce the separator, resulting in an ISC [9]. In addition, the introduction of other impurity particles in the production of electrode materials or electrode lug burr caused by the improper cutting of electrode pieces may also trigger ISCs [10].

ISC would usually need to go through a long evolution before developing (i.e., deteriorating) into a thermal runaway [11]. In its early stage (which is known as the micro short circuit, or MSC), due to the large equivalent short-circuit resistance, the changes in electrical and thermal parameters of batteries caused by an MSC are not noticeable, which implies that the MSC fault is difficult to be detected [12]. With the gradual reduction of the equivalent short-circuit resistance, faults in the late ISC stage can induce significant abnormal changes in the battery electro-thermal parameters, which is manifested as a sudden voltage drop and steep temperature rise within a short period of time, with intense and concentrated heat release [13]. In this case, the detection and early warning of ISC faults in lithium-ion batteries is relatively easy to achieve. However, the time scale of the evolution from the late-stage ISC to thermal runaway is on the order of milliseconds, which means that once the late-stage ISC failure is detected, it would be too late for any control and management measures to respond effectively [14]. Therefore, timely diagnosis of MSC faults is a critical and challenging task to ensure the safe and stable operation of lithium-ion batteries.

Existing MSC diagnosis methods for lithium-ion battery packs can be divided into two categories: qualitative detection and quantitative diagnosis. Qualitative detection methods include correlation-based methods [15,16], charging voltage ranking (CVR) based method [17], and data-driven methods [18–20]. Correlation-based methods detect MSC cells by calculating either the correlation coefficient of terminal voltage or that of state of charge (SOC) between adjacent cells in the battery pack. Specifically, if the correlation coefficients between a cell and its two adjacent cells are lower than a predetermined threshold, the cell may be in an MSC state [15,16]. The CVR-based method relies on that fact that the CVR of normal cells within the battery pack should remain constant, whereas an MSC causes the voltage of the faulty cell to drop and thus alters its rank in the CVR, in that observing such changes is able to facilitate detection and locating MSC cells [17]. Taking terminal voltage and electrochemical impedance spectrum as inputs and the probability of MSC as output, the data-driven method detects battery MSC based on statistical learning algorithms such as principal component analysis [18], kernel principal component analysis [19], or deep neural network [20].

The aforementioned qualitative detection methods can determine the occurrence of MSCs within battery packs and identify the faulty cell. However, these methods are unable to quantitatively calculate the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance, thereby lacking a quantitative description of the severity of the short circuit. Indeed, short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance, which serve as scientific indicators for assessing the severity and evolution stages of MSCs, are instrumental in enabling battery management systems to devise tailored response strategies. Consequently, researchers have proposed a range of quantitative diagnostic methods.

The existing quantitative diagnostic methods calculate the short-circuit current by computing the temporal rate of change of the battery's leakage electric quantity (LEQ), which is caused by an MSC. Subsequently, the short-circuit resistance is further determined using Ohm's law to quantitatively describe the severity of MSC faults. Based on different principles for calculating LEQ, quantitative diagnostic methods can be classified as methods based on: (1) the mean-difference model [21–23]; (2) representative cell state [24,25]; (3) charging features [26,27]; and (4) Coulomb counting [28,29].

The methods based on the mean-difference model utilize an equivalent circuit model (ECM) to establish a mean model, which represents the average performance of the battery pack. Simultaneously, using the ECM, a difference model is established for each individual cell to characterize the disparities between the state of that cell and the average state of the battery pack. These disparities include differences in open-circuit voltage, internal resistance, among others. Based on the mean-difference model, the difference between the SOC of a single cell and the average SOC of the battery pack can be estimated by using filtering algorithms such as the extended Kalman filtering. Multiplying the SOC difference by the battery pack's capacity yields the individual cell's LEQ [21–23]. The methods based on representative battery state utilize the sorting of terminal voltages among individual cells to select the cell with the median terminal voltage as a representative of the normal cells within the battery pack. The SOC difference between each remaining cell and the representative cell is subsequently computed. The cell's LEQ is obtained by multiplying the cell's capacity with the SOC difference [24,25].

The approach based on charging features extracts characteristics, such as the remaining charging capacity, from the data at the constant-current charging stage to represent the LEQ caused by an MSC [26,27]. The Coulomb counting method employs the ECM and the extended Kalman filter to estimate the battery's SOC, while simultaneously utilizing the SOC calculated through Coulomb counting as a reference standard. The LEQ is computed by comparing the SOC estimated by the filter with the SOC calculated through Coulomb counting [28,29]. In summary, the current quantitative diagnostic methods rely on the SOC differences or the remaining charge capacity to calculate the short-circuit current and the short-circuit resistance.

However, the existing quantitative diagnostic methods are confronted by two main limitations. One of those is that these methods are susceptible to the interference of cell-to-cell inconsistencies within the battery pack, which in turn leads to misidentification of MSC cells. More specifically, imperfections in the manufacturing process of batteries can lead to inconsistencies in intrinsic parameters among individual cells [30]. Furthermore, during subsequent operations, variations in cell location and ventilation conditions can lead to disparities in temperature characteristics and degradation rates among individual cells within the battery pack [31]. These factors inevitably lead to inconsistencies in SOC and aging state between the individual cells. Therefore, the short-circuit resistance, as recognized by existing quantitative diagnostic methods, may simply be originated from the inconsistencies among the cells, rather than being an indicator for MSC cells. For instance, within a series-connected battery pack, lower-capacity cells and MSC cells exhibit similar SOC transient characteristics during the discharge process. Therefore, existing methods based on SOC differences may misdiagnose lower-capacity cells as MSC cells and vice versa.

The second limitation is that the existing methods suffer from low computational efficiency. Since the location of the MSC cells cannot be determined in advance, the existing quantitative diagnosis method needs to calculate one by one the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance of all cells in a battery pack. For instance, the methods based on the mean-difference model require establishing difference models for all individual cells within the battery pack. The model parameters of all cells need to be identified, and from there, the SOC differences of all cells can be calculated. Subsequently, the short-circuit currents and resistances of all cells are computed. Only then can the determination be made regarding which cells are in an MSC state. However, practically, calculating indiscriminately the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance of all individual cells in a battery pack lacks specificity, and implies a large number of unnecessary calculations burdening the battery management system. This is particularly problematic for battery packs composed of hundreds or thousands of individual cells, in which the number of cells experiencing MSC simultaneously is only a tiny fraction of the total number of cells. Therefore, how to improve the accuracy and computational efficiency of MSC fault diagnosis for battery pack under the interference of cell inconsistency is an urgent problem to be addressed.

In this regard, this work proposes an efficient MSC fault detection and quantitative diagnostic method that not only takes into account cell inconsistencies for lithium-ion battery packs, but also can circumvent the tedious cell-by-cell calculation of short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance. Firstly, the normal, inconsistent, and MSC cells within the battery pack are isolated based on the incremental capacity (IC) correlation analysis. For the detected MSC cell, an ECM under MSC is established. Based on this, an alternating iterative algorithm is designed to calculate the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance of the MSC cell based on a recursive least squares algorithm with forgetting factor (FFRLS) and an adaptive H ∞ Kalman filtering (AHKF). Finally, to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, an MSC simulation experiment with variable short-circuit resistance is performed on a battery pack consisting of cells with different initial SOCs and different aging states. The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows.
(1)
Using the median IC as a reference standard, accurate classificiation of normal cells, MSC cells, and inconsistent cells within the battery pack is achieved based on time- and frequency-domain IC correlation;
(2)
The designed alternating iterative algorithm enables online calculation of short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance without the need for horizontal comparison of state differences between cells, thereby completely eliminating the interference caused by cell inconsistency;
(3)
The proposed diagnostic method does not need to calculate the short-circuit current and resistance for all cells within the battery pack, thus alleviating the computational burden on the battery management system.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 details the diagnostic method proposed in this work. Section 3 presents the battery pack MSC simulation experiments and data acquisition considering cell inconsistency. Section 4 provides the analysis and discussion of the diagnostic results. Finally, Section 5 concludes the paper.

In the absence of MSCs and under the condition where the aging status and initial SOC parameters among individual cells remain consistent, the constant current charging voltage curves among the cells should be overlapped, resulting in a well-maintained consistency among the obtained IC curves of the cells [32]. Conversely, inconsistencies in the aging state and initial SOC between cells can lead to shifts or scaling of the charging voltage curve in magnitude and time scale, which in turn affects the trend and shape of the IC curves. When an MSC occurs, the battery needs to discharge the short-circuit resistance during the charging process, and the energy of the battery is continuously consumed, which further leads to a difference between the IC curve of the MSC cell and the IC curves of the normal cells.

Based on the above principle, the overall framework of the MSC detection and quantitative diagnosis algorithm for lithium-ion battery packs proposed in this paper is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The algorithm takes the current of the battery pack and the terminal voltages of each cell as inputs. The outputs of the algorithm include the number of normal cells, inconsistent cells, and MSC cells, as well as the short-circuit resistance of the MSC cell. The implementation process of the algorithm consists of four main steps.

Firstly, the terminal voltages between the individual cells in the battery pack are ranked in ascending order at any moment of the constant-current charging phase to obtain the median terminal voltage. The median IC curve can be derived based on the obtained median terminal voltage. In actual operation, for a battery pack consisting of hundreds or thousands of cells, the number of MSC and inconsistent cells will be very unlikely to exceed half of the total number of cells in the pack. As such, taking into account that the median represents the 50 percentile of the samples, the median IC almost surely corresponds to the state of normal a cell within the battery pack. Therefore, it is used as a reference benchmark to detect MSC cells and inconsistent cells within the battery pack. Secondly, the time-domain correlation coefficient is computed between the IC curves of individual cells and the median IC curve, followed by a comparison with a predetermined threshold to distinguish those normal cells from the anomalous ones, which may be MSC cells or inconsistent cells. Subsequently, for the anomalous cells, the frequency-domain correlation coefficients are calculated between their IC-curve spectra and the median IC-curve spectrum, followed by a comparison with a predetermined threshold to determine whether the anomalous cell exhibits an MSC fault or parameter inconsistency. Finally, the ECM under MSC is established for the detected MSC cell. An alternating iterative algorithm based on FFRLS and AHKF is designed to calculate the short-circuit current online and update the short-circuit resistance based on Ohm's law. The detailed implementation process of MSC detection and quantitative calculation of short-circuit resistance is elaborated in Section 2.1 and Section 2.2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MSC cells based on IC correlation
ce:section.ce:para: In this subsection, based on IC correlation analysis, we employ the median IC as the reference benchmark to detect normal cells, inconsistent cells, and MSC cells within the battery pack. The IC of each cell and the median IC are first derived from the terminal voltage of each cell and the median terminal voltage, respectively. The Spearman correlation coefficient is employed for quantitative analysis of the IC correlations. The detection process and criteria can be derived based on the above process.

IC analysis is a nondestructive technique to identify the degradation mechanism of lithium-ion batteries. In this work, we derive the IC curve based on data obtained during the constant-current charging phase. By calculating the derivative of the charge capacity with respect to the cell voltage during the constant current charging phase, the voltage plateau of the charge voltage curve due to the electrode phase change can be converted into an intuitive and recognizable peak on the IC curve. Numerical differentiation is often used to obtain the IC curve, which is calculated as follows:

where Q, V, and i denote the charging capacity, terminal voltage, and current of the battery in constant current charging mode, respectively. Eq. (1) represents the calculation of IC based on an equal time interval Δt, where V 2 ​− ​V 1 represents the voltage change during the time interval Δt. Eq. (2) represents the calculation of IC based on an equal voltage interval ΔV, where Q 2 ​− ​Q 1 signifies the change in charge capacity within the voltage interval ΔV. In this paper, the IC is calculated based on the equal voltage interval method. ΔV is set to 1 ​mV in order to sufficiently capture the characteristics of the IC curve.

The IC curve obtained through the aforementioned numerical differentiation method is susceptible to measurement noise. Therefore, it is necessary to employ appropriate filters to obtain a smooth IC curve. In this paper, the moving average filtering is employed to smooth the IC curve. A moving average filter smooths the signal by calculating the average value of the signal within a preset window. Given a time-varying signal s contaminated by noise, the moving average filter can be designed as:

After obtaining the median and individual-cell IC curves, we quantitatively analyze the correlation between the IC curve of each cell and the median IC curve in both the time and frequency domains, to detect MSC cells and inconsistent cells. Specifically, this paper analyzes the IC correlation based on the Spearman correlation coefficient.

The Spearman correlation coefficient is a nonparametric measure of the rank correlation of two variables. Let a and b be two random variables, and the i th values of a and b are a
i and b

In the detection process of MSC cells and inconsistent cells, the Spearman correlation coefficient between the median IC curve and the IC curve of each cell is first calculated in the time domain. If the time-domain IC correlation coefficient r st of a specific cell is less than the predefined threshold r thr1, it indicates that the cell is in an abnormal state, which means that it could potentially be an MSC cell or an inconsistent cell. Otherwise, the cell is classified as a normal cell, and its identifier is output. Next, the IC curves of the anomalous cells and the median IC curve are subjected to fast Fourier transform (FFT) to transform the IC data into the frequency domain. Furthermore, the Spearman correlation coefficient between the spectrum of the IC curve of each abnormal cell and the spectrum of the median IC curve is calculated in the frequency domain. If the frequency-domain IC correlation coefficient r sf of an abnormal cell is smaller than a predetermined threshold r thr2, it indicates that the cell is an MSC cell, otherwise, the cell is an inconsistent cell. Consequently, the detection of normal cells, MSC cells, and inconsistent cells within the battery pack can be accomplished.

Similar to its role in the domains of signal processing [34] and image processing [35], the FFT is utilized in this research to extract certain features that contribute to the isolation of MSC cells and inconsistency cells. It should be noted that the aforementioned process is purely data-driven, and the FFT of the IC curves does not have a specific physical interpretation. But the absence of a mechanistic explanation does not affect the application of the proposed method and the integrity of this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alternating iterative algorithm for quantitative calculation of MSC resistance
ce:section.ce:para: After detecting and locating the MSC cell, an ECM for the MSC cell is established based on real-time measurements of the battery pack current and the terminal voltage of the MSC cell. An alternating iterative algorithm, designed using FFRLS and AHKF, is then employed to calculate the short-circuit resistance online. The detailed implementation process is described as follows.

Considering the general trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity, the Thevenin model is employed in this study to capture the MSC fault characteristics of lithium-ion batteries. During the occurrence of MSC in a cell, the short-circuit resistance continuously dissipates the battery's energy, and this process can be effectively represented by a parallel connection of a short-circuit resistance across the battery. Therefore, the adopted battery model under MSC is depicted in Fig. 2 , where U oc is the open-circuit voltage of the battery. I denotes the current flowing inside the battery. R 0 represents the internal resistance of the battery. R p and C p represent the polarization resistance and polarization capacitance, respectively. U p is the voltage drop across the polarization resistance. I L indicates the external load current, which can be measured in real-time by the current sensor. R sc represents the MSC resistance. I sc is the MSC current, which is caused by R sc and is unmeasurable during the actual operation of the battery pack. Finally, U t represents the terminal voltage of the battery.

From Kirchhoff's law, the dynamic behavior of the ECM under MSC in Fig. 2 can be expressed as:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Online update of model parameters based on the FFRLS algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: At the initial moment of an MSC, the electro-thermal characteristics of the MSC cell do not exhibit significant changes. Therefore, a relatively large initial value can be assigned to the short-circuit resistance R sc. The model parameters, such as α, R p, R 0, or the open-circuit voltage U oc, are then updated online based on the current of the battery pack and the terminal voltage of the MSC cell measured in real-time.

In the state-space model represented by Eq. (25), the state and observation equations of the system are linear, so Kalman filtering is a potential scheme for estimating the state vector x
k . The prerequisite for accurately estimating the system state using Kalman filtering is to have prior knowledge of the statistical characteristics of system noise and the system model parameters. Specifically, the matrices A
k , B
k , C
k , D
k , Q
k , and R
k in the state-space model (25) need to be accurately identified, and Q
k and R
k should remain constant throughout the computation process. However, it can be observed from Eq. (25) that the system matrices of the state-space model of the MSC cell contain the short-circuit resistance R sc. The random initialization of R sc inevitably leads to uncertainty in the model parameters and introduces errors with unknown statistical properties. The AHKF algorithm [37] utilizes the H ∞ criterion from robust control to address system uncertainty and effectively handles the system noise with unknown statistical characteristics by adaptively updating the covariance matrices Q
k for process noise and R
k for measurement noise. This approach mitigates the adverse effects of model uncertainty on estimation results and enables rapid convergence of the state estimates to the true values. Therefore, in this paper, the AHKF algorithm is employed to estimate the short-circuit current I sc of the MSC cell by the following steps.

By utilizing the recursive formulas of the AHKF algorithm as mentioned above, the short-circuit current I sc can be estimated. Subsequently, the short-circuit resistance is updated based on
R
sc
=
U
t
I
sc
. The updated short-circuit resistance R sc is substituted into the ECM of the MSC cell, and the online update of the model parameters and the short-circuit current calculation are performed again based on FFRLS and AHKF. The above process is repeated until the fluctuation of the short-circuit resistance is less than a predetermined threshold, which means that the estimated short-circuit resistance converges to the actual value and can be output.

The alternating iterative algorithm designed in this paper is summarized as Algorithm 1. It is worth noting that, except for the initial time step, for any time step k, the value of R init is set as the estimated short-circuit resistance from the previous time step k − 1. Based on Algorithm 1, it can be deduced that the computation of the short-circuit resistance of MSC cells does not necessitate lateral comparison of the state differences between cells. Consequently, the interference caused by the inconsistency among cells can be completely eliminated. Furthermore, since MSC cells can be detected in advance, this study only calculates the short-circuit resistance for MSC cells. Unlike existing quantitative diagnostic methods for MSCs that perform indiscriminate calculations for all cells within the battery pack, the proposed approach in this study exhibits strong targeting capability, which can circumvent unnecessary calculations and alleviate the calculation burden of the battery management system.
Fig. 9

ce:section.ce:section-title: MSC triggering experiment and data acquisition
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the characteristics of MSC faults in lithium-ion batteries and validate the proposed diagnostic method, it is necessary to trigger MSC faults in the cells within the battery pack. The existing short-circuit triggering methods include mechanical abuse triggering such as acupuncture or extrusion, triggering by implanting impurity particles or temperature-controlled materials inside the battery, and triggering by inducing dendrite growth through electrical abuse such as overcharge and over-discharge. Indeed, these methods are primarily used to simulate later-stage internal short-circuit faults, which can pose safety risks and result in battery explosions within a short period of time [15]. As a result, the controllability and repeatability of these methods are relatively poor. In this study, the MSC fault is simulated by connecting external resistors in parallel across the battery. This method can flexibly simulate different evolutionary stages of short circuits by adjusting the size of the resistance value and has better controllability and repeatability.

The experimental setup is depicted in Fig. 3 (a). All experiments are performed at a constant temperature of 25 °C provided by a thermal chamber. The host computer is responsible for controlling the battery test system to charge and discharge the battery. During the experimental process, the data such as the terminal voltage of cells, the temperature of cells, or the current of the battery pack, are collected by the battery testing system and transmitted in real-time to the host computer. The data sampling frequency is set to 1 ​Hz.

The battery pack selected for the experiment consists of 16 cylindrical 21700-type lithium-ion battery cells connected in series. The specifications of the cell are shown in Table 1 . The capacity inconsistency among the cells is evaluated using the discharge capacity of each cell, which is measured under the 1 ​C constant current discharge at an ambient temperature of 25 °C. In order to investigate the characteristics of MSC faults in lithium-ion battery packs under the presence of cell-to-cell inconsistencies, prior to cell grouping, cyclic aging is performed on cell 5 and cell 13 to degrade their capacities to 3.85 Ah and 3.69 Ah, respectively, while the remaining cells are new and not subjected to aging. Simultaneously, cell 3 and cell 11 are subjected to constant current charging to set their initial SOC at 35% and 50%, respectively, while the initial SOC of the remaining cells is set at 20%. After introducing the inconsistency among the cells, the initial SOC and capacity of each cell are presented in Table 2 .

The current profiles for charging and discharging used in the experiment are depicted in Fig. 3(d). The battery pack is charged at a constant current of 0.5 ​C, and the charging process is terminated when the maximum terminal voltage of any cell reaches the charging cut-off voltage of 4.2 ​V to prevent overcharging. Subsequently, a dynamic stress test (DST) discharging is conducted on the battery pack. The discharging process is terminated when the minimum terminal voltage of any cell reaches the discharging cut-off voltage of 2.75 ​V to prevent over-discharging of the cells. A total of 9 charge–discharge cycles are performed on the battery pack.

Fig. 3(b) shows the physical view of the series-connected battery pack placed inside the constant temperature chamber during the charge–discharge process, while Fig. 3(c) depicts the corresponding electrical connection diagram of the series-connected battery pack. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b) and (c), to simulate the evolution process of MSC, the short-circuit resistors of different magnitudes and corresponding manual switches are first connected in series and then collectively connected in parallel to the cell 7 and cell 15. By closing different switches, short-circuit resistors of different magnitudes can be connected in parallel to the battery to simulate the occurrence and evolution of MSCs. The smaller the short-circuit resistance, the higher the short-circuit current, which indicates a more severe short-circuit event. Therefore, in order to simulate different stages of MSCs, we select short-circuit resistors with amplitudes ranging from large to small: 1,000 ​Ω, 750 ​Ω, 500 ​Ω, 300 ​Ω, 200 ​Ω, and 100 ​Ω.

During the initial three charge–discharge cycles of the battery pack, all switches remain in the open state, which indicates the absence of MSCs within the battery pack. From the fourth to ninth charge–discharge cycles, the corresponding switches are closed sequentially to connect short-circuit resistors in decreasing magnitudes across the cell 7 and cell 15. This experimental approach aimed to emulate the gradual deterioration of MSCs within the battery pack as the charge–discharge process advanced. For instance, at the onset of the 4th charge–discharge cycle, a short-circuit resistor of 1,000 ​Ω is connected in parallel to the cell 7 and cell 15 by closing the switches s7,1 and s15,1 while keeping the other switches in open circuit. Similarly, at the beginning of the 9th charge–discharge cycle, a short-circuit resistor of 100 ​Ω is connected in parallel to the cell 7 and cell 15 by closing the switches s7,6 and s15,6 while leaving the other switches in open circuit. The switch states and parallel short-circuit resistors corresponding to each charge–discharge cycle are presented in Table 3 .

Based on the battery pack current and the terminal voltage of each cell collected in Section 3, the median and individual-cell IC curves can be derived under different charge–discharge cycles by using the IC calculation method and moving average filtering in Section 2.1.1. Subsequently, the identification of normal, inconsistency, and MSC cells within the battery pack can be achieved through the IC-correlation-based detection discussed in Section 2.1.3. For the detected MSC cells, the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance are calculated using the alternating iterative algorithm proposed in Section 2.2. All experiments are performed on a computer equipped with an Intel-i7 3.3 ​GHz CPU and 16 ​GB RAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IC curves obtained at different severity of MSC
ce:section.ce:para: The median and individual-cell IC curves under different charge–discharge cycles are illustrated in Fig. 4 . From Fig. 4, it can be observed that the IC curves of the normal cells overlap consistently during the charging process under different short-circuit resistances. Furthermore, the median IC curve consistently overlaps with the IC curves of the normal cells during each charging process, which confirms the assumption that the median IC curve can serve as a representative of the normal cell state within the battery pack and can thus be used as a reference standard for detecting inconsistent cells and MSC cells within the battery pack.

As compared to the IC curves of the normal cells, the IC curves of cells 3 and 11 with higher initial SOC exhibit a rightward shift in the starting point and peak position, and the magnitude of the shift increases with higher initial SOC. This is because during the charging process, the cells with higher initial SOC are fully charged first and their terminal voltage reaches the charging cutoff voltage earlier than the terminal voltage of normal cells. At this point, the battery pack stops charging to prevent the cell from overcharging, while the other cells remain in a partially charged state. In each charge–discharge cycle, the peak values of the IC curves for aged cells 5 and 13 are lower than that of a normal cell. This can be attributed to the low capacity of the aged cells, which results in a fast rise in charging voltage and SOC and a short duration of the voltage plateau during the constant current charging process.

In the initial three charge–discharge cycles, since there is no MSC in the battery pack, the IC curves of other cells except the inconsistent cells 3, 5, 11 and 13 are superimposed and maintain good consistency. Starting from the fourth cycle, the IC curves of the MSC cells 7 and 15 exhibit a leftward shift in the starting point and peak position compared to the IC curves of other cells. Moreover, the magnitude of this shift increases gradually with the decrease in the equivalent short-circuit resistance. This phenomenon can be attributed to the continuous energy consumption of the MSC cells by the short-circuit resistance during the charging process. As a result, the charging voltages and SOCs of the MSC cells increase at a slower rate compared to other cells. When the battery pack ceases charging, the MSC cells remain in a partially charged state with the highest remaining charge capacity. This effect becomes more pronounced as the short-circuit resistance decreases and the short-circuit current increases.

From Section 2.1.1, it is evident that the acquisition of the IC curves, based on the data obtained during the constant current charging phase, only requires the utilization of Eqs. (2) and (3). This process entails no complex computations, thereby resulting in relatively low computational requirements. The time required to obtain the IC curves for all individual cells under different charge-discharge cycles is presented in Table 4 . It is evident that the magnitude of the time required to acquire the IC curves for all individual cells is on the order of one-hundredth of a second across various charge–discharge cycles. Additionally, as the short-circuit resistance decreases, indicating a worsening of MSC conditions, the required time exhibits a decreasing trend. This also demonstrates that the computational requirement for calculating the IC curves is relatively low. Therefore, the algorithm proposed in this paper enables rapid computation of the IC curves for batteries, making it feasible for online operation.

Firstly, the Spearman correlation coefficient is calculatd between the median IC curve and the IC curve of each individual cell in the time domain. By observing the calculation results, it is found that the minimum value of the correlation coefficient corresponding to normal cells is 0.98 within the initial three charge–discharge cycles when no MSC occurs within the battery pack. Therefore, we set the thresholds r thr1 and r thr2 to 0.98 during the detection process. It is worth noting that in the actual operation of lithium-ion battery packs, the thresholds r thr1 and r thr2 need to be calibrated through offline testing. They can be set to different values and flexibly adjusted according to practical circumstances to enhance the sensitivity of the algorithm.

The time-domain IC correlation coefficients of each cell under different charge–discharge cycles and different short-circuit resistances are illustrated in Fig. 5 , where the horizontal coordinate indicates the cell index and the vertical coordinate indicates the time-domain IC correlation coefficient for each cell. The purple dashed lines with arrows at both ends indicate the preset threshold values of the time-domain IC correlation coefficients. As shown in Fig. 5, during the initial three charge–discharge cycles, the time-domain IC correlation coefficients of cells 3, 5, 11, and 13 are below the predetermined threshold value r thr1 ​= ​0.98, whereas the time-domain IC correlation coefficients of the remaining cells are greater than or equal to r thr1. Starting from the 4th charge–discharge cycle, the time-domain IC correlation coefficients of cells 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, and 15 are less than r thr1. Conversely, from the 4th to 9th charge–discharge cycles, the time-domain IC correlation coefficients of the remaining cells, excluding the aforementioned ones, are higher than the predetermined threshold. Therefore, according to the abnormal cell detection criteria proposed in Section 2.1.3, within the initial three charge–discharge cycles, cells 3, 5, 11, and 13 are diagnosed as abnormal cells, and the remaining cells are normal. During the 4th to 9th charge–discharge cycle, cells 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, and 15 are diagnosed as abnormal cells, and the remaining cells are judged as normal cells without any abnormality. Note that the detected abnormal cells at this stage could be either cells with MSCs or cells with inconsistencies.

After detecting the abnormal cells within the battery pack, the IC curves of these abnormal cells and the median IC curve are subjected to FFT. Naturally, the Spearman correlation coefficient between the spectrum of the median IC curve and the spectrum of the IC curve of each abnormal cell is calculated in the frequency domain. The obtained frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients for each abnormal cell are illustrated in Fig. 6 , where the red dashed lines with arrows at both ends indicate the predefined threshold r thr2 ​= ​0.98. As shown in Fig. 6, the frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients of abnormal cells 3, 5, 11, and 13 are all above r thr2 throughout all charge–discharge cycles. Therefore, according to the criteria proposed in Section 2.1.3, abnormal cells 3, 5, 11, and 13 are identified as inconsistent cells. On the contrary, starting from the 4th charge–discharge cycle, the frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients of abnormal cells 7 and 15 are lower than the preset threshold, and with the decrease of short-circuit resistance and the deterioration of short-circuit severity, the frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients exhibit a decreasing trend. Hence, during the 4th to 9th charge–discharge cycles, abnormal cells 7 and 15 are identified as MSC cells. It is worth noting that, as shown in Fig. 6, during the initial three charge–discharge cycles, the frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients of cells 7 and 15 exceed the predetermined threshold, which indicates that cells 7 and 15 did not experience MSCs during the three initial cycles. In fact, according to the aforementioned detection results based on time-domain IC correlation coefficients, cells 7 and 15 have been accurately identified as normal cells in the initial three charge–discharge cycles.

In summary, the above-mentioned detection results are in full accordance with the actual conditions set in Section 3, thereby demonstrating the capability of the proposed detection method to accurately distinguish normal cells, inconsistent cells, and MSC cells within the battery pack. This serves as conclusive evidence for the effectiveness and feasibility of the detection method proposed in this study.

Upon detecting the MSC cell within the battery pack during the constant current charging phase, the current of the battery pack and the terminal voltage of the MSC cell during the discharging phase are input into the alternating iterative algorithm designed in Section 2.2 for quantitative calculation of the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance. The values of the hyperparameters in the calculation process are summarized in Table 5 . In addition, the proposed alternating iterative algorithm is compared to the method based on Coulomb counting and extended Kalman filtering (denoted as CC-EKF) proposed in the literature [29] and the method based on ECM and H∞ nonlinear observer (denoted as ECM-H) proposed in the literature [28]. The root mean square error (RMSE) is adopted as the evaluation metric to assess the performance of different diagnostic methods. The calculation of RMSE is as follows
(43)

where A
t , E
t , and n represent the actual value of the short-circuit resistance at time instant t, the estimated value of the short-circuit resistance at time instant t, and the number of observation points, respectively.

The results of estimating the short-circuit current of the MSC cells 7 and 15 by the proposed method under different charge–discharge cycles are demonstrated in Fig. 7 , where the local enlargements of the corresponding position are below the curve. From Fig. 7, it can be observed that in all charge–discharge cycles, the estimated values of the short-circuit currents for cell 7 and cell 15 quickly converge to the actual values after approximately 500 ​s of oscillation. Moreover, the estimated values consistently align well with the actual values during the subsequent discharge process. Fig. 8 presents the results of estimating the short-circuit resistances for MSC cells 7 and 15 using the proposed method in various charge–discharge cycles. As can be observed in Fig. 8, the estimated short-circuit resistance values of cell 7 and cell 15 converge quickly to the actual values after a brief period of minor oscillations in all charge–discharge cycles.

The RMSEs of the results obtained by the three methods for estimating the short-circuit resistance of cell 7 and cell 15 are listed in Tables 6 and 7
, respectively. From Tables 6 and 7, it can be concluded that the proposed method in this study achieves the lowest RMSE under different charge–discharge cycles. This finding demonstrates the capability of our method to achieve a more accurate quantitative diagnosis for MSCs with varying degrees of severity. In addition, it can also be noted from Tables 5 and 6 that as the equivalent short-circuit resistance decreases, the RMSE of the results obtained by the three methods exhibits a decreasing trend, which can be attributed to the fact that the diagnostic algorithm can better capture the MSC fault characteristics of the battery as the MSC gradually deteriorates and the electro-thermal parameters of the battery change more and more significantly.

The above analysis indicates that the proposed alternating iterative algorithm in this study can accurately calculate the short-circuit resistance of MSC cells, thereby facilitating the quantification of the severity of MSCs. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed quantitative diagnostic method for MSCs.

The main aim of this study is to perform MSC fault detection and severity quantification for cells in lithium-ion battery packs. One essential novelty is that the proposed method factors in the inter-cell inconsistency, which is often misdiagnosed as MSC fault. The distinction between MSC cells and inconsistent cells is made possible through an IC correlation analysis. As for the quantification for fault severity, an alternating iterative algorithm is proposed to estimate the short-circuit current and short-circuit resistance for the detected MSC cells. In short, the following conclusions can be drawn.
(1)
The median IC curve serves as a good proxy in defining the state of normal cells in a battery pack. This is a general observation, in that, the median IC curve calculated based on the sorted terminal voltage consistently overlaps with the IC curves of normal cells in multiple charge–discharge cycles;
(2)
IC correlation analysis enables the accurate isolation of inconsistent cells and MSC cells. In the context of frequency-domain IC correlation analysis, MSC cells consistently exhibit frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients lower than a predefined threshold. In contrast, inconsistent cells consistently exhibit frequency-domain IC correlation coefficients greater than or equal to that predefined threshold;
(3)
Regarding the detected MSC cells, the estimated short-circuit resistance through the designed alternating iterative algorithm exhibits rapid convergence to the true values, which substantiates that the proposed method can achieve the accurate quantitative diagnosis of MSCs. This capability is instrumental in enabling the battery management system to accurately discern the evolutionary stage and severity of MSCs, thereby facilitating the development of tailored response strategies.

In the process of MSC fault detection, the setting of the threshold has a significant impact on the detection results, and therefore, it needs to be selected in accordance with the aging of batteries and the evolution of MSC faults to improve detection accuracy. In future works, the investigation of techniques for dynamically updating the thresholds to adapt to the evolving characteristics of MSC faults and the aging of batteries should be carried out.
The data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
